title: Model Cards for Model Reporting
summary: Introduces model cards as structured documentation for intended use, performance characteristics, limitations, and ethical considerations.
auditUse: Use when designing model documentation evidence and checking whether performance claims are broken down by relevant conditions.
citation: Mitchell et al. 2019. Model Cards for Model Reporting. FAT* '19.

title: Datasheets for Datasets
summary: Proposes a standardized process for documenting datasets, including motivation, composition, collection, uses, and maintenance.
auditUse: Use to structure dataset evidence requests and test whether data provenance and limitations are documented.
citation: Gebru et al. 2021. Datasheets for Datasets. Communications of the ACM.

title: Closing the AI Accountability Gap: Defining an End-to-End Framework for Internal Algorithmic Auditing
summary: Defines an internal algorithmic auditing framework across scoping, mapping, artifact collection, testing, and reflection.
auditUse: Use as a backbone for audit program design across the AI development lifecycle.
citation: Raji et al. 2020. Closing the AI Accountability Gap. FAccT '20.

title: AI Auditing: The Broken Bus on the Road to AI Accountability
summary: Taxonomizes current AI audit practices and examines why many audits fail to produce meaningful accountability outcomes.
auditUse: Use to evaluate whether an audit design has institutional context, stakeholder power, and accountability consequences.
citation: Birhane et al. 2024. AI Auditing: The Broken Bus on the Road to AI Accountability. arXiv.

title: A Framework for Assurance Audits of Algorithmic Systems
summary: Proposes a criterion audit framework modeled partly on financial audit assurance practices.
auditUse: Use to think about audit criteria, evidence quality, auditor responsibility, audit opinions, and certification.
citation: Lam et al. 2024. A Framework for Assurance Audits of Algorithmic Systems. FAccT '24.

title: Outsider Oversight: Designing a Third Party Audit Ecosystem for AI Governance
summary: Synthesizes lessons from external oversight systems in other fields to design better AI third-party audit ecosystems.
auditUse: Use when evaluating independence, audit market structure, disclosure, incentives, and third-party access.
citation: Raji et al. 2022. Outsider Oversight. AIES.

title: Sociotechnical Audits: Broadening the Algorithm Auditing Lens to Investigate Targeted Advertising
summary: Extends algorithm auditing by examining interactions between systems and users, not only model inputs and outputs.
auditUse: Use when auditing deployed systems where human behavior, platform context, and downstream effects matter.
citation: Lam et al. 2023. Sociotechnical Audits. arXiv.

title: Governing with Algorithmic Impact Assessments: Six Observations
summary: Examines how algorithmic impact assessments can operate as governance mechanisms and where practice gaps remain.
auditUse: Use to compare audit work with impact assessment and to shape pre-deployment review processes.
citation: Moss et al. 2020. Governing with Algorithmic Impact Assessments. SSRN.

title: Assessing the Assessment: Comparing Algorithmic Impact Assessments and AI Audits
summary: Compares audits and impact assessments as complementary governance practices with different accountability functions.
auditUse: Use to decide when the site should route a resource toward audit, impact assessment, or both.
citation: Sloane and Moss. 2023. Assessing the Assessment. SSRN.

title: Fairness and Abstraction in Sociotechnical Systems
summary: Explains abstraction traps that arise when technical fairness work ignores social and institutional context.
auditUse: Use as a guardrail against fairness audits that over-rely on metrics without context.
citation: Selbst et al. 2019. Fairness and Abstraction in Sociotechnical Systems. FAT* '19.

title: Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning
summary: Introduces equal opportunity and equalized odds criteria for supervised learning fairness.
auditUse: Use when reviewing group fairness metrics and their limits in model evaluation.
citation: Hardt, Price, and Srebro. 2016. Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning. NeurIPS.

title: Fairness Beyond Disparate Treatment and Disparate Impact: Learning Classification without Disparate Mistreatment
summary: Introduces disparate mistreatment as a fairness concept based on misclassification rates across groups.
auditUse: Use when checking whether an audit considers error-rate disparities, not only selection-rate disparities.
citation: Zafar et al. 2017. Fairness Beyond Disparate Treatment and Disparate Impact. WWW '17.

title: Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems
summary: Frames ML systems as sources of hidden technical debt from data dependencies, entanglement, feedback loops, and configuration issues.
auditUse: Use to identify operational control risks in production ML systems.
citation: Sculley et al. 2015. Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems. NeurIPS.

title: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models
summary: Presents HELM as a broad evaluation framework for language models across scenarios and metrics.
auditUse: Use when designing LLM evaluation coverage beyond a single benchmark score.
citation: Liang et al. 2022. Holistic Evaluation of Language Models. arXiv.

title: Measuring Massive Multitask Language Understanding
summary: Introduces the MMLU benchmark for measuring broad multitask language understanding.
auditUse: Use as an example of benchmark design and as a reminder to inspect benchmark relevance to the audited use case.
citation: Hendrycks et al. 2021. Measuring Massive Multitask Language Understanding. ICLR.

title: Red Teaming Language Models to Reduce Harms: Methods, Scaling Behaviors, and Lessons Learned
summary: Reports methods and lessons from red teaming language models to discover harmful behavior.
auditUse: Use when planning adversarial testing, harm discovery, and red-team evidence collection.
citation: Ganguli et al. 2022. Red Teaming Language Models to Reduce Harms. arXiv.

title: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback
summary: Describes training AI assistants using constitutional principles and AI feedback rather than direct human labels for every harmful output.
auditUse: Use when reviewing alignment claims, policy-to-model translation, and evidence for harmlessness controls.
citation: Bai et al. 2022. Constitutional AI. arXiv.

title: Explaining and Harnessing Adversarial Examples
summary: A foundational paper explaining adversarial examples and adversarial training.
auditUse: Use to ground robustness testing and adversarial-risk discussions.
citation: Goodfellow, Shlens, and Szegedy. 2015. Explaining and Harnessing Adversarial Examples. ICLR.

title: Membership Inference Attacks Against Machine Learning Models
source: IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy
summary: Demonstrates black-box membership inference attacks that reveal whether records were used in model training.
auditUse: Use when auditing model privacy leakage, API exposure, and training data protection.
citation: Shokri et al. 2017. Membership Inference Attacks Against Machine Learning Models. IEEE S&P.

title: Concrete Problems in AI Safety
summary: Taxonomizes accident-risk problems such as negative side effects, reward hacking, scalable oversight, safe exploration, and distribution shift.
auditUse: Use to frame failure modes and monitoring questions in AI risk assessments.
citation: Amodei et al. 2016. Concrete Problems in AI Safety. arXiv.

title: Stop Explaining Black Box Machine Learning Models for High Stakes Decisions and Use Interpretable Models Instead
summary: Argues that high-stakes decisions should prefer interpretable models over post-hoc explanations of black boxes where feasible.
auditUse: Use when challenging explainability claims and evaluating whether opaque models are justified.
citation: Rudin. 2019. Stop Explaining Black Box Machine Learning Models. Nature Machine Intelligence.

title: A Survey of Methods for Explaining Black Box Models
summary: Surveys methods for explaining black-box models and categorizes explanation approaches.
auditUse: Use to choose explanation methods and understand their limits before accepting explainability evidence.
citation: Guidotti et al. 2018. A Survey of Methods for Explaining Black Box Models. ACM Computing Surveys.

title: On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?
summary: Analyzes risks of large language models, including environmental cost, data practices, bias, and deployment harms.
auditUse: Use when scoping broader LLM risks beyond benchmark performance.
citation: Bender et al. 2021. On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots. FAccT '21.

title: The Path to Consensus on Artificial Intelligence Assurance
summary: Frames AI assurance as a consensus-building discipline for demonstrating that AI systems satisfy required properties.
auditUse: Use to explain why audit evidence, assurance cases, standards, and shared vocabulary matter for trustworthy AI.
citation: Freeman et al. 2022. The Path to Consensus on Artificial Intelligence Assurance. Computer.

title: A Survey on Artificial Intelligence Assurance
summary: Surveys AI assurance concepts, assurance cases, verification, validation, trust, explainability, fairness, and governance concerns.
auditUse: Use as a broad orientation paper for the assurance section of the library.
citation: Batarseh, Freeman, and Huang. 2021. A Survey on Artificial Intelligence Assurance. Journal of Big Data.

title: Toward Trustworthy AI Development: Mechanisms for Supporting Verifiable Claims
summary: Proposes mechanisms for supporting verifiable claims about AI development practices and system properties.
auditUse: Use to connect claims, evidence, documentation, third-party review, and institutional accountability.
citation: Brundage et al. 2020. Toward Trustworthy AI Development. arXiv.

title: The Malicious Use of Artificial Intelligence: Forecasting, Prevention, and Mitigation
source: Future of Humanity Institute / CSET / OpenAI / others
summary: Analyzes how AI capabilities can be misused across digital, physical, and political security domains.
auditUse: Use when adding misuse and threat-modeling sections to AI audit scoping.
citation: Brundage et al. 2018. The Malicious Use of Artificial Intelligence. arXiv.

title: Frontier AI Regulation: Managing Emerging Risks to Public Safety
summary: Outlines regulatory approaches for managing public safety risks from frontier AI systems.
auditUse: Use when mapping frontier model governance controls to policy proposals and public safety obligations.
citation: Anderljung et al. 2023. Frontier AI Regulation. arXiv.

title: Managing Extreme AI Risks Amid Rapid Progress
summary: Argues for risk management, governance, technical safety work, and institutional preparation for extreme AI risks.
auditUse: Use when building the site's frontier-risk and severe-harm audit material.
citation: Bengio et al. 2024. Managing Extreme AI Risks Amid Rapid Progress. Science.

title: The AI Risk Repository: A Comprehensive Meta-Review, Database, and Taxonomy of Risks From Artificial Intelligence
summary: A meta-review and database organizing hundreds of AI risks into a reusable taxonomy.
auditUse: Use as a master risk taxonomy for audit scoping, control mapping, and glossary expansion.
citation: Slattery et al. 2024. The AI Risk Repository. Patterns.

title: A Taxonomy of Systemic Risks From General-Purpose AI
summary: Develops a taxonomy of systemic risks from general-purpose AI, including malicious use, accidents, structural risks, and governance failures.
auditUse: Use when expanding audit scope beyond individual model behavior to ecosystem-level risks.
citation: Uuk et al. 2024. A Taxonomy of Systemic Risks From General-Purpose AI. arXiv.

title: AI Risk Categorization Decoded: Unveiling a Comprehensive Framework for AI Risk Assessment
summary: Compares and organizes AI risk categorization schemes into a comprehensive framework.
auditUse: Use when harmonizing different risk taxonomies across standards, regulations, and internal policies.
citation: Zeng et al. 2024. AI Risk Categorization Decoded. arXiv.

title: An Overview of Catastrophic AI Risks
summary: Surveys catastrophic AI risk categories, including malicious use, accidents, and structural risks.
auditUse: Use to seed severe-harm scenarios and frontier-risk audit prompts.
citation: Hendrycks, Mazeika, and Woodside. 2023. An Overview of Catastrophic AI Risks. arXiv.

title: Unsolved Problems in ML Safety
summary: Organizes unresolved ML safety problems across robustness, monitoring, alignment, and systemic safety concerns.
auditUse: Use when turning safety research gaps into audit questions and test coverage categories.
citation: Hendrycks et al. 2021. Unsolved Problems in ML Safety. arXiv.

title: Model Evaluation for Extreme Risks
summary: Proposes model evaluation as a governance tool for identifying and managing extreme risks from advanced AI systems.
auditUse: Use to design evidence requirements for dangerous capability and alignment evaluations.
citation: Shevlane et al. 2023. Model Evaluation for Extreme Risks. arXiv.

title: Evaluating Frontier Models for Dangerous Capabilities
summary: Presents approaches for evaluating frontier models for dangerous capabilities relevant to public safety.
auditUse: Use for capability-evaluation checklists and frontier model release-gate evidence.
citation: Phuong et al. 2024. Evaluating Frontier Models for Dangerous Capabilities. arXiv.

title: Foundational Challenges in Assuring Alignment and Safety of Large Language Models
summary: Reviews challenges in assuring the alignment and safety of LLMs, including evaluation, specification, monitoring, and governance limitations.
auditUse: Use when defining what assurance can and cannot currently prove about LLM behavior.
citation: Anwar et al. 2024. Foundational Challenges in Assuring Alignment and Safety of Large Language Models. arXiv.

title: DecodingTrust: A Comprehensive Assessment of Trustworthiness in GPT Models
summary: Evaluates GPT models across trustworthiness dimensions including toxicity, bias, adversarial robustness, privacy, and ethics.
auditUse: Use when designing multi-dimensional LLM audit batteries.
citation: Wang et al. 2023. DecodingTrust. NeurIPS.

title: TrustLLM: Trustworthiness in Large Language Models
summary: Introduces a benchmark and taxonomy for evaluating trustworthiness in LLMs.
auditUse: Use to broaden LLM audit coverage across truthfulness, safety, fairness, robustness, privacy, and ethics.
citation: Huang et al. 2024. TrustLLM. ICML.

title: MACHIAVELLI: A Benchmark for Evaluating Agent Harmfulness
summary: Benchmarks harmful behavior in language agents through decision-making scenarios.
auditUse: Use when assessing agentic AI systems for harmful incentives, deception, and unsafe behavior.
citation: Pan, Bhatia, and Steinhardt. 2023. MACHIAVELLI. ICML.

title: AuditMAI: Towards Systematic Multi-Agent LLM Auditing via Adaptive and Interpretable Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Proposes a multi-agent framework for adaptive and interpretable auditing of LLM behavior.
auditUse: Use as a forward-looking reference for automated audit assistants and LLM audit workflows.
citation: Waltersdorfer, Elliott, and Gottlob. 2024. AuditMAI. arXiv.

title: Operationalising AI Governance Through Ethics-Based Auditing: An Industry Case Study
summary: Examines ethics-based AI auditing as a way to operationalize AI governance in industry.
auditUse: Use as a practical case study for moving from AI principles to audit practice.
citation: Mökander and Floridi. 2022. Operationalising AI Governance Through Ethics-Based Auditing. AI and Ethics.

title: Continuous Auditing of Artificial Intelligence: A Conceptualization and Assessment of Tools and Frameworks
summary: Conceptualizes continuous AI auditing and assesses tools and frameworks that can support ongoing assurance.
auditUse: Use to design monitoring-oriented audit programs rather than one-time reviews.
citation: Mökander and Floridi. 2023. Continuous Auditing of Artificial Intelligence. Digital Society.

title: The Case for a Broader Approach to AI Assurance: Addressing Hidden Harms in the Development of Artificial Intelligence
summary: Argues that AI assurance must address hidden harms and development-process risks, not only narrow technical performance.
auditUse: Use as a reminder to audit development practices, organizational incentives, and overlooked stakeholder harms.
citation: Stinson et al. 2024. The Case for a Broader Approach to AI Assurance. AI & Society.

title: Towards Algorithm Auditing: Managing Legal, Ethical and Technological Risks of AI, ML and Associated Algorithms
summary: Discusses algorithm auditing as a response to legal, ethical, and technological risks in AI and machine learning systems.
auditUse: Use when translating legal and ethical AI risk into audit procedures.
citation: Kazim et al. 2024. Towards Algorithm Auditing. SSRN.

title: Auditing Algorithms: Research Methods for Detecting Discrimination on Internet Platforms
source: Data and Discrimination: Converting Critical Concerns into Productive Inquiry
summary: A foundational methods paper for auditing algorithmic systems and detecting discrimination on platforms.
auditUse: Use to teach black-box audit designs, sock-puppet audits, scraping audits, and platform accountability methods.
citation: Sandvig et al. 2014. Auditing Algorithms.

title: Algorithmic Accountability Reporting: On the Investigation of Black Boxes
summary: Develops investigative methods for holding algorithmic systems accountable despite opacity.
auditUse: Use to connect algorithm auditing with investigative methods, transparency gaps, and public accountability.
citation: Diakopoulos. 2015. Algorithmic Accountability Reporting.

title: Accountability of AI Under the Law: The Role of Explanation
summary: Analyzes how explanation can support legal accountability for AI decisions.
auditUse: Use when reviewing explanation requirements, contestability, and audit evidence for high-stakes decisions.
citation: Doshi-Velez et al. 2017. Accountability of AI Under the Law.

title: AI Fairness 360: An Extensible Toolkit for Detecting, Understanding, and Mitigating Unwanted Algorithmic Bias
summary: Introduces an open-source toolkit for bias detection, explanation, and mitigation.
auditUse: Use as a reference for fairness metric implementation and practical bias audit tooling.
citation: Bellamy et al. 2018. AI Fairness 360. arXiv.

title: Aequitas: A Bias and Fairness Audit Toolkit
summary: Describes a toolkit for auditing machine learning models for bias and fairness across groups.
auditUse: Use when designing fairness audit workflows and result reporting.
citation: Saleiro et al. 2018. Aequitas. arXiv.

title: Gender Shades: Intersectional Accuracy Disparities in Commercial Gender Classification
summary: A landmark audit showing intersectional accuracy disparities in commercial gender classification systems.
auditUse: Use as a canonical example of external AI auditing and subgroup performance analysis.
citation: Buolamwini and Gebru. 2018. Gender Shades. PMLR.

title: Fairlearn: A Toolkit for Assessing and Improving Fairness in AI
summary: Describes Fairlearn's approach to assessing and improving fairness in AI systems.
auditUse: Use as a practical reference for fairness assessment tooling and mitigation workflows.
citation: Bird et al. 2020. Fairlearn. Microsoft Research.
